title: Walnut charcuterie board
subtitle: A small face-grain walnut serving board for cheese and cured meats
excerpt: A serving board in English or American black walnut, 300 mm by 200 mm and 20 mm thick. The board is planed flat, the edges are eased with a block plane, and the surface is finished with raw linseed oil. The project introduces face-grain planing and the linseed food-safe finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the serving board construction and finish; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the cheese and charcuterie board form.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber dried to equilibrium moisture content. A seasoned walnut board will not warp or crack when used as a serving surface.
The broad, flat surface of a board where the annual rings run across the face. A face-grain board shows the full figure of the wood and is suitable for serving use.
The surface exposed when a board is cut across the width of the log. End-grain absorbs oil deeply and is harder than face-grain.
Raw linseed oil is a food-safe penetrating finish that hardens slowly in the wood fibres. Used for chopping boards and serving surfaces.

content.text: Walnut is one of the best hardwoods for a serving board: it has a fine closed grain that resists moisture absorption, a natural oil in the fibres that provides some inherent protection, and a dark colour that shows less staining than maple or beech. A
board is not intended for heavy chopping; it is for serving and will last a lifetime with simple
walnut is available from hardwood suppliers in 20 to 25 mm thickness.

A board at 300 mm long, 200 mm wide, and 20 mm thick. English walnut has a finer figure than American black walnut; both are food-safe and excellent for this purpose. Inspect the board for end splits: any crack that runs more than 20 mm in from the end of the board should be cross-cut away before starting. Confirm the board is dry: if it feels cold and heavy compared to a piece held for a few minutes, it may still be green inside.

A roughsawn board needs both faces planed flat before use. Check with a straight edge: any hollow of more than 0.5 mm across the face needs planing. Set the block plane to a fine cut. Plane in the direction of the

; look at the surface sheen to identify the correct direction before each pass. Planing against the grain produces a rough torn surface. Check with the straight edge after each five passes until both faces are flat.
Saw the board to 300 mm by 200 mm with the tenon saw. Ease the long edges with the block plane: three light passes at 45 degrees to remove the sharp arris. The
short edges are sanded with 320-grit on the sanding block, working from the face edges toward the centre of each end to avoid breakout.
Sand all faces to 320-grit along the grain direction. Apply a generous coat of raw

oil with a cloth, covering all faces including the end grain. Allow it to soak for thirty minutes; wipe off all surface oil thoroughly. Any oil left on the surface will gum rather than harden. Allow to dry for forty-eight hours. Apply a second coat by the same method. Three coats total before first use is the standard.

Wash by hand and dry immediately; never leave the board submerged or in a dishwasher. Re-oil with a few drops of raw linseed oil worked in by hand when the board starts to look dry or dull, typically every two to three months with regular use.

attrs.symptom: The surface is rough after planing
attrs.fix: Look at the surface sheen after a test pass in each direction. Planing with the grain gives a continuous ribbon-like sheen; against the grain gives a broken, rough texture. Always plane in the smoother direction.

attrs.symptom: The linseed oil finish is sticky after 24 hours
attrs.cause: Too much oil left on the surface
attrs.fix: Wipe down the surface firmly with a dry cloth to remove all uncured oil. Sticky oil on the surface gums rather than hardening. After wiping, leave for another 24 hours in a warm room.
